title: Existing Platforms
summary: Existing platforms are large third-party networks — App Stores, browser extension stores, social platforms, marketplaces like Amazon and Craigslist — that already aggregate millions of potential users. The channel works by riding someone else's distribution: filling gaps, earning features and rankings, and launching early on new platforms before they saturate. The upside is rapid scale without building your own audience. The downside is platform risk — you don't own the relationship, and a policy change can end the channel overnight.

pretest.prompt: Every founder knows 'put the app on the App Store.' But treating existing platforms as a full traction channel means something sharper than 'distribute on Apple.' Before reading: what structurally distinguishes 'existing platforms' as a channel from other channels that also use third-party properties (BD, affiliate, targeting blogs)?
pretest.modelAnswer: The defining property is that you build a product, feature, or app that lives *inside* a third-party network's distribution surface — App Store, browser extension store, Shopify app store, Facebook platform, Chrome Web Store, Amazon, Craigslist, Twitter. The platform's own discovery mechanics (rankings, featured slots, search, API integration points) become your primary acquisition channel. This differs from BD (custom-negotiated partnerships with deliverables), affiliate (posted performance-based payouts), and targeting blogs (placed content on someone's site) because the platform treats you as one of many interchangeable participants subject to its rules, rankings, and algorithms. You don't negotiate with Apple for a feature; you earn it through ratings and quality. You don't pay Chrome to promote your extension; you rank in its store. The trade is: access to millions of users already assembled by the platform, in exchange for operating entirely on the platform's terms — which can change unilaterally.

lesson.markdown:
## The definition

The existing-platforms channel means **deploying your product (or a purpose-built extension of it) inside a third-party network that already aggregates your users, and leveraging that network's native distribution mechanics to acquire them.**

The load-bearing phrase is *native distribution mechanics*. You're not buying ads on the platform; you're not negotiating a partnership with the platform owner; you're not placing guest content. You are shipping something that lives inside the platform's surface — an iOS app, a Chrome extension, a Shopify app, a Facebook app, a Craigslist-integrated listing, a Twitter-client feature — and you acquire users through the platform's own discovery machinery: rankings, featured slots, search, recommendations, permission dialogs, API entry points.

## What counts as 'an existing platform'

The category is broader than most founders assume. Useful groupings:

- **App Stores.** Apple App Store, Google Play, Amazon Appstore. Hundreds of millions of users, category rankings, editorial features.
- **Browser extension stores.** Chrome Web Store, Firefox Add-ons, Edge, Safari. Smaller audiences but much less competition per install.
- **SaaS marketplaces.** Shopify App Store, Salesforce AppExchange, Atlassian Marketplace, Slack App Directory, Zoom Marketplace. Highly targeted: you meet users who already own the host product.
- **Social platforms.** Facebook, Twitter/X, Pinterest, TikTok, Instagram, LinkedIn. Platform apps (old Facebook era) or platform-native content/features.
- **Marketplaces and classifieds.** Amazon, eBay, Etsy, Craigslist. Either selling inside them or routing their users back to your product.
- **Content platforms.** YouTube, Reddit, Tumblr, Medium, Substack. Presence as a content creator can be a channel in itself, blurring into content marketing (Ch 13).

What they all share: a large, pre-assembled user base, a discovery mechanism the platform controls, and a set of integration points a third party can build against.

## Why the channel exists at all

Platforms benefit from third-party developers in three ways: (1) a richer product for their users, (2) reduced engineering burden (they don't have to build every feature), (3) ecosystem lock-in (apps built on the platform raise the cost of switching). This alignment of incentives is why platforms expose APIs, run app stores, and run ranking systems in the first place. Your job as a founder is to build something whose existence makes the platform more valuable to its users — that's what the platform's algorithms and editors are trying to surface.

## Three structural roles

Every existing-platforms play has:

- **The platform owner.** Apple, Google, Shopify, Meta, Amazon. Sets the rules, controls distribution, takes a cut (0–30% typical), can change terms unilaterally.
- **The platform user.** The audience already assembled on the platform. Their trust in the platform transfers partially to apps inside it.
- **The third-party builder (you).** Ships an app, extension, or integration. Competes with other third parties for ranking, features, and discovery.

The channel's most important asymmetry is that the platform owner always has leverage over you — they can change APIs, ranking rules, revenue splits, or deplatform you entirely. This is the single biggest structural difference from channels you control (SEO, content, email).

## What it is not

- **Not paid ads on a platform.** Running Facebook or TikTok ads is social ads (Ch 10), not existing platforms. The distinction: ads buy impressions; platforms channel leverages native discovery and integration.
- **Not BD.** BD negotiates a custom deal with a specific partner. Platforms channel takes a posted, public integration surface and competes with everyone else on it.
- **Not engineering as marketing.** Eng-as-marketing (Ch 15) builds free standalone tools as lead magnets. Platforms channel puts your product *inside* someone else's existing ecosystem.
- **Not simply 'having an app.'** If you ship an iOS app but acquire every user through paid social or PR, you're using other channels. Platforms channel specifically means acquiring through the platform's own discovery.

## Why it's often the right early channel

For consumer apps and anything with a platform-native form factor, existing platforms can deliver disproportionate scale early because they give you access to users who would otherwise cost $5–$50 to reach individually through paid channels. The Zyngas, Instagrams, Socialcams, and Evernotes of the world built user counts in the millions partly by being in the right place during the right platform moment — Facebook in 2008, iOS in 2009, Android in 2010. The channel rewards early builders willing to bet on emerging platforms before saturation.

workedExample.setup: Amira runs Snapcurrent, a 14-person startup making a currency-conversion and travel-expense tracking utility. Her team is deciding what 'existing platforms' would mean as a Bullseye channel for them, as opposed to paid social or content.
workedExample.walkthrough:
**Step 1 — List plausible platforms.** Amira's team brainstorms every surface where 'someone booking or on a trip' might be discovered:
- iOS App Store (obvious — travelers use phones)
- Google Play (same)
- Chrome Web Store (a browser extension that converts prices on any ecommerce site)
- Shopify App Store (convert prices for international shoppers — but that's a different product)
- Slack App Directory (per-channel currency bot for remote-first teams)
- Pixel-perfect Apple Watch complication (niche but iOS-native)

**Step 2 — Distinguish this from adjacent channels.** A colleague suggests Facebook ads targeting travelers. Amira pushes back: 'That's social ads, not platforms. Platforms means we ship something inside an Apple or Google surface and compete for a featured slot.' Another colleague suggests negotiating with Booking.com to be the default converter in their app. 'That's BD, not platforms. Platforms means a posted API we can build against without negotiation.'

**Step 3 — Pick a platform with structural fit.** Of the six candidates, the Chrome Web Store stands out:
- Travelers comparison-shop in browsers
- The Chrome store has well-understood ranking mechanics (installs, ratings, reviews)
- Competition is thinner than iOS (maybe 20 currency extensions vs 400 currency apps)
- The extension can auto-convert prices on any site, which is inherently shareable

**Step 4 — Identify the native discovery mechanic.** On Chrome Web Store: category rankings, search keywords ('currency converter,' 'USD to EUR,' 'travel'), editor-featured extensions, and 'related extensions' suggestions. Getting into the top 10 of 'currency converter' search would drive ~800 daily installs based on a rough benchmark she pulled from a similar tool's public stats.

**Step 5 — Plan the build-and-launch.** Amira's team scopes a 4-week build of a Chrome extension that (a) auto-detects foreign prices on any webpage and converts to the user's home currency, (b) integrates with the Snapcurrent mobile app via a shared account, (c) prompts for a 5-star rating after the user has converted 10+ prices. They plan to seed the first 500 installs via their existing email list, ask all of them for reviews, then monitor the Chrome store search ranking for their target keywords.

**What makes this a valid 'platforms' play.**
- They're building inside a third-party distribution surface (Chrome Web Store).
- Acquisition flows primarily through native discovery (search, rankings, featured).
- It's not ads, not BD, not affiliate.
- The surface is the user's actual context of need (browsing an international site).

**What would have made this NOT platforms.** If her plan had been 'pay $15k to an influencer to promote the extension,' that's PR / influencer. If it had been 'write SEO blog content about FX rates,' that's content marketing. The platforms label is earned by depending on the platform's own discovery engine.

contrast.explanation: BD and existing platforms are both third-party channels, but they differ in contract structure and power asymmetry. BD involves a negotiated bilateral deal — custom deliverables, shared metrics, mutual commitment. Existing platforms means opting into a public API or submission process where you're one of thousands of participants subject to the platform's rules, with essentially zero negotiating power. A second axis: BD partners typically share incentives with you (both sides want the deal to work). Platform owners are indifferent to your specific success — they care about their ecosystem aggregate and can change rules that hurt you without consulting you. Rule of thumb: if you're on a call negotiating terms, it's BD; if you're submitting to a review queue, it's existing platforms.

flashcards.front: What is the load-bearing phrase in the definition of the existing-platforms channel, and what does it imply?
flashcards.back: 'Native distribution mechanics.' You acquire users through the platform's own discovery engine — rankings, features, search, recommendations, API entry points — not through ads, BD, or placed content on the platform. You build inside the platform's surface and compete on its terms.

flashcards.front: Name six groupings of existing platforms relevant to startups.
flashcards.back: (1) App Stores (Apple, Google Play). (2) Browser extension stores (Chrome, Firefox). (3) SaaS marketplaces (Shopify, Salesforce, Slack, Atlassian). (4) Social platforms (Facebook, Twitter, Pinterest, TikTok). (5) Marketplaces and classifieds (Amazon, eBay, Etsy, Craigslist). (6) Content platforms (YouTube, Reddit, Medium).

flashcards.front: Name the three structural roles in an existing-platforms play and the power asymmetry between them.
flashcards.back: (1) Platform owner — sets rules, controls distribution, takes a cut, can change terms unilaterally. (2) Platform user — the pre-assembled audience. (3) Third-party builder (you) — competes for ranking, features, and integration. Power asymmetry: the platform owner always has leverage over the builder; they can change APIs, rankings, or deplatform you.

flashcards.front: Paid ads on a platform = ______ channel (Ch 10). Posting a Chrome extension to the Web Store and earning rankings = ______ ______ channel. The distinction is impressions-bought vs. native-discovery-earned.
flashcards.back: social ads ; existing platforms

flashcards.front: A founder says 'we're using the existing platforms channel because we have an iOS app.' Is this correct?
flashcards.back: Not necessarily. Having an iOS app is a precondition; using existing platforms as a channel means acquiring users through the App Store's own discovery — rankings, featured slots, category pages, search — rather than through paid social, PR, or other external channels that happen to route to the store. Many apps exist on iOS without using the App Store as a channel.

scenarios.setup: A founder building a B2B analytics tool says: 'Our channel strategy is Salesforce AppExchange. We'll negotiate a co-marketing deal with Salesforce's partnerships team to feature us.'
scenarios.question: What's being conflated here, and how should they sharpen their strategy?
scenarios.modelAnswer: The founder is conflating two channels that happen to involve the same logo. AppExchange listing (with rankings, reviews, category visibility, search) is the existing-platforms channel. Negotiated co-marketing is BD. They can pursue both, but they require different teams, different timelines, and different measures of success. Sharpen: (1) Treat AppExchange as a platforms play — focus on earning reviews, writing a listing that ranks, building a free tier that drives installs, and hitting the quality bar for editorial features. Typical timeline: 6–18 months to meaningful install volume. (2) Treat the Salesforce partnerships conversation as BD — it's a negotiated deal with deliverables, and it may or may not happen; Salesforce has limited partnership slots. Typical timeline: 3–9 months of relationship-building. (3) Don't confuse them in reporting — AppExchange installs are platform-driven; co-marketing leads are BD-driven. Mixing them obscures which play is actually working. The founder should also recognize that Salesforce-the-platform-owner has limited interest in their specific success on the AppExchange; they're one of thousands of apps, and rankings will be earned through normal ecosystem dynamics, not via the partnerships team.
rubric: Identifies the two-channel conflation
Separates platforms (listing) from BD (co-marketing)
Names distinct measures of success for each
Notes different timelines
Points out platform-owner indifference to individual builder
Recommends not mixing them in reporting

scenarios.setup: A founder of a new note-taking app insists: 'existing platforms is our channel because we ship to the App Store.' But 100% of their installs come from a paid TikTok creative campaign. Their organic App Store ranking is #900 in Productivity.
scenarios.question: Are they using the existing-platforms channel? What should they do to start?
scenarios.modelAnswer: They are not using existing platforms as a channel — they're using paid social (TikTok) that happens to route through the App Store for fulfillment. The App Store is a storefront for them, not a discovery engine. True existing-platforms use would mean a non-trivial share of installs coming from organic App Store discovery: category browsing, search for productivity-related keywords, editorial features, 'similar apps' suggestions. Since their organic ranking is #900, the platform's own discovery is delivering ~nothing. To start actually using the channel: (1) Audit their current App Store listing — screenshots, keywords, title, subtitle, description. App Store Optimization (ASO) is the minimum viable use of the platforms channel. (2) Pick a narrower keyword where ranking in the top 10 is realistic ('focus notes for students,' not 'note-taking'). (3) Drive an initial installs-and-ratings burst to climb the ranking for that keyword, using their existing TikTok traffic as the jumpstart. (4) Build pitches for editorial features and submit them to App Store editors. (5) Only then measure: what percent of new installs are coming from organic App Store discovery? The target metric for saying 'we use existing platforms as a channel' should be something like 30%+ of installs originating in the store itself, not routed from other channels.
rubric: Distinguishes storefront from discovery engine
Sets a measurable threshold for 'using the channel'
Prescribes ASO as the starting point
Recommends keyword narrowing to a winnable term
Names editorial-feature pitches as an action
Specifies the correct success metric (share of organic installs)

scenarios.setup: A Shopify-app founder argues existing platforms is a high-leverage channel but refuses to invest in it because 'Shopify could change the rules at any time and kill us.' A co-founder argues this is true of every channel.
scenarios.question: Who's right, and what's the nuanced position?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Both are partly right, but the first founder understates channel specificity. Every channel has some risk (Google can change SEO rules, Facebook can change ad rules, emails can go to spam). But platform risk on existing platforms is structurally more concentrated than most other channels for three reasons: (1) The platform owner can change integration APIs overnight, breaking your product outright. (2) The platform's ranking and feature algorithms are opaque and can shift without notice. (3) You have essentially no contractual protection — terms of service are updatable, and the platform owner owes you nothing. Compare to paid ads: Facebook can raise CPMs, but your business still functions at the new price point. Compare to SEO: Google can tweak the algorithm, but you can adapt. On a platform, a single policy change can eliminate your product category (see: Apple's restrictions on certain parental-control apps, Facebook's graph API shutdown in 2015, Twitter's API changes in 2023). Nuanced position: platforms are often a great early channel precisely because they're easier to grow on than channels you own, but they should rarely be your only channel by year 2 — build platform-native acquisition early, then use the scale you gain to invest in channels you control (SEO, content, email, your own direct acquisition). The first founder is right to take the risk seriously; they're wrong to refuse the upside. The co-founder is right that risk exists everywhere; they're wrong that all channel risks are equivalent.
rubric: Acknowledges both positions have merit
Identifies three specific reasons platform risk is concentrated
Contrasts with paid ads and SEO risk profiles
Names real historical examples of platform-change damage
Prescribes the 'platforms early, own-channels later' strategy
Rejects both the 'refuse entirely' and 'equal risk' positions

teachback.prompt: Explain the existing-platforms channel to a founder who thinks 'we have an iOS app' is the same as 'we use existing platforms.' Cover: (1) the load-bearing phrase 'native distribution mechanics' and what it implies, (2) the six main groupings of platforms, (3) the three structural roles and the power asymmetry, (4) what the channel is NOT (paid ads, BD, eng-as-marketing, simply shipping an app), and (5) why it's often a strong early channel despite the platform-risk downside.
rubric: Defines the channel via 'native distribution mechanics'
Names at least five platform groupings
Identifies all three roles and the power asymmetry
Distinguishes from at least three adjacent channels
Articulates why it's attractive early
Acknowledges platform risk without letting it dominate

concepts.title: The Native Discovery Mechanism — Rankings, Features, and the Build-Early Advantage

pretest.prompt: When platforms work as a traction channel, why do they work — mechanically? What are the feedback loops and platform dynamics that turn a small initial bump into outsized user acquisition, and why does 'be early' matter so much?
pretest.modelAnswer: The core mechanism is a reinforcing ranking loop: installs (or usage) → higher ranking in the platform's discovery surfaces → more impressions from organic users browsing those surfaces → more installs → higher ranking, repeat. The loop compounds because platforms actively promote whatever is already popular; popularity is causal, not just correlated. A secondary mechanism is editorial features: platforms' editors pick a small number of apps to promote on homepages, category pages, or 'best of' lists; being featured produces a 10–100× install spike that often pushes the app into a higher ranking tier, which self-sustains. 'Be early' matters because (a) on a new platform, competition is thin and the ranking loop can be triggered with modest initial installs; (b) platform owners actively market new platforms, and apps that are there on day one benefit from that marketing for free; (c) editorial features are more available on new platforms because editors need apps to highlight; (d) API/feature access on new platforms is broader and less restricted than on mature platforms. Combined: a modest app on a new platform can achieve rankings that an excellent app on a mature platform cannot, purely because of the competitive density.

lesson.markdown:
## The ranking loop

All major platform discovery surfaces — App Store rankings, Chrome Web Store rankings, Shopify App Store rankings, Amazon product rankings — run on a reinforcing loop. It's the same shape every time:

1. You acquire some initial installs or conversions from outside the platform (paid ads, PR, email list, a shitty click-through test).
2. Installs + ratings + recency feed the platform's ranking algorithm.
3. You move up in category or keyword rankings.
4. More users browsing the platform see you because you're higher.
5. Organic installs increase.
6. Higher installs feed the ranking algorithm further.
7. Loop repeats until a ceiling (category saturation, competitor response, or your quality-drop) stops it.

Mark Johnson (Focused Apps) described this loop in 2011 and it still describes App Store and most marketplace dynamics today. The loop is *reinforcing*, which means small initial advantages can compound into large positional advantages. It's also *pricing-dependent on quality*, because if your ratings drop, the loop runs backward.

## Featured slots as step-function events

Beyond algorithmic rankings, most platforms have *editorial* promotion — human curators picking apps to feature on homepages, category pages, seasonal promotions, and 'editor's choice' lists. Features are step-function events: a single feature can produce a 10–100× install spike over a single day or week, which often pushes the app into a higher ranking tier that persists after the feature ends.

Trainyard's story (the iOS puzzle game) is the canonical illustration. A blog mention pushed it to #1 free in Italy, which pushed it up in the UK. Seven days after its free version launched, Apple featured it on the main App Store page, and the install graph 'completely blew up.' The editorial feature didn't just add installs on the feature week — it locked in a higher organic ranking that continued producing installs long after the feature ended.

Platforms use features to reward apps that (a) are high quality, (b) showcase new platform capabilities, (c) fit current editorial themes (seasonal, categorical), (d) have high ratings. Apps that are *designed* with featured-slot-fit in mind — apps that use a new API the platform wants to promote, apps that exemplify the platform's design language — get features more often than equally good apps that don't.

## Filling gaps in the platform itself

A second mechanism, distinct from rankings, is *gap filling*. Every platform has things its users need that the platform itself hasn't built. The canonical examples:

- **YouTube ⇄ MySpace (2005–2006):** MySpace had no native video hosting. YouTube shipped embeddable video, MySpace users embedded YouTube videos in their profiles, and every embed was a recruiting link back to YouTube.
- **Bit.ly ⇄ Twitter (2008–2010):** Twitter's 140-character limit made long URLs hostile. Bit.ly shipped short URLs, became the default shortener on Twitter, and acquired users purely through Twitter's own content flow.
- **Imgur ⇄ Reddit (2009+):** Reddit had no native image hosting. Imgur shipped a zero-friction image host, became the default, and rode Reddit's growth for years.

The mechanism: you build the feature the platform users need, the platform users insert your product into the platform's native content, and every piece of platform content becomes a distribution surface for you. The platform gets a better user experience; you get users. This works best on platforms that either don't want to build the missing feature themselves (often because it's not strategic for them) or haven't gotten around to it yet.

## The build-early advantage

Evernote is the paradigm example of a company that built its distribution primarily via 'be first on every new platform.' As Alex Pachikov put it: 'Every year there's a new platform, new device, new something… you risk that all that effort is a waste. But when a platform is popular, it's crowded.' The mechanism behind build-early has four parts:

1. **Thinner competition.** On day one of a new platform, you're one of the only apps in your category. The ranking loop fires with modest initial installs because there's nothing else for users to discover.
2. **Free platform marketing.** Platforms heavily promote themselves at launch — keynotes, ad campaigns, retail push. Apps present on day one get mentioned or included in that marketing.
3. **Editorial hunger.** Platform editors need apps to feature. On day one, the pool of candidates is tiny. Build something half-decent that shows off the new platform and editors will feature you.
4. **API access.** Early-platform APIs are often broader and less restricted than mature-platform APIs. You can build things that later builders won't be able to.

Evernote executed this on the iPhone (day one), iPad (day one with a custom iPad version, not just a port), Android (early, featured six weeks), Kindle Fire (early), and Verizon Android (piggybacked on Verizon's own marketing). The downside: they also bet on Nokia, Windows Phone, and Blackberry, all of which flopped. The math worked anyway because the winners' upside dwarfed the losers' wasted engineering.

## Why the math works (even with platform flops)

Build-early is structurally positive-EV for startups because the cost of being early on a flop-platform is modest (weeks of engineering) while the cost of being late on a winner-platform is enormous (you never catch the leaders who were there at launch). If 1 in 5 new platforms succeed, and success delivers 100× the users of failure, build-early is a winning bet on expectation — as long as you're also building on multiple platforms and not betting your entire company on one.

## The ceiling conditions

The ranking-loop + features + gap-filling machine doesn't run forever. The mechanism breaks when:

- **The platform saturates.** Mature platforms have thousands of apps per category. Initial-install requirements to trigger the loop become prohibitive.
- **The platform changes rules.** Apple can restrict certain categories; Google can deprioritize add-on apps; Facebook can shut down its graph API. When the platform changes rules, every builder loses simultaneously.
- **Your quality drops.** If ratings fall, rankings fall, and the loop runs backward.
- **The gap gets filled natively.** If Twitter builds its own URL shortener, Bit.ly's Twitter-driven growth ends. If MySpace builds native video, YouTube's embed-driven growth ends (though by then YouTube had escape velocity).

workedExample.setup: Devon is about to ship a Slack app for async standup reports. His team is planning the launch. They have a $12k budget for the launch month, an email list of 1,800 people interested in async work, and a small backing of engineers willing to produce custom marketing content. He's trying to design a launch that specifically triggers the native discovery mechanisms on the Slack App Directory.
workedExample.walkthrough:
**Step 1 — Understand the Slack Directory's discovery surfaces.** Devon audits what drives installs inside the Slack App Directory:
- Search (for 'standup,' 'async,' 'daily report')
- Category pages (Productivity, HR)
- 'Featured' section on Directory homepage
- 'Essential Apps' lists curated by Slack editors
- 'New & Notable' carousel for recently launched apps
- 'More apps like X' suggestions on other apps' pages

**Step 2 — Design for the ranking loop.** Installs, ratings, and Slack-verification status feed ranking. Plan: (a) Seed 1,500+ installs in week one from the email list to hit an install threshold. (b) Aggressively collect 5-star reviews (ask every active user after day 3). (c) Complete Slack verification before launch so he earns the verified badge, which improves ranking and trust.

**Step 3 — Design for featured slots.** Features go to apps that (a) use new Slack APIs or features, (b) have polished onboarding, (c) showcase the platform well. Plan: (a) Build a deep integration with Slack Canvas and new workflow-builder APIs (showcases platform capabilities). (b) Film a 45-second Loom of the setup flow showing it takes under 60 seconds — attach it to a cold email to the Slack partnerships/editorial team. (c) Submit through Slack's 'Nominate your app for featuring' form with a polished one-pager.

**Step 4 — Design for gap-filling.** What do Slack users need that Slack doesn't build? Devon picks two angles: (a) Async standups specifically for distributed teams (Slack's own Workflow Builder handles this clunkily). (b) Rollup reports that post to channels automatically on a schedule with AI-generated team summaries. Slack is unlikely to build team-summary AI natively any time soon — this is the genuine gap.

**Step 5 — Build-early angle.** Slack just released a new 'Agents & Assistants' API for integrating AI agents directly into channels. It's three months old, relatively few apps use it yet. Devon decides to ship the AI-powered rollup report feature as an Agent, not a bot — which positions him for any Slack marketing push around the Agents launch and increases editorial interest.

**Step 6 — Fire the loop.**
- Week 1: Launch to email list → 1,620 installs. Slack verification complete. Submit nomination for featuring.
- Week 2: Active in-app review prompts → 284 reviews, 4.8 average.
- Week 3: Rank #8 in Productivity for 'standup' search. Organic installs: 90/day.
- Week 4: Slack editorial team responds to nomination, puts Devon in the 'New & Notable' carousel for two weeks.
- Week 6: 'New & Notable' feature drives 4,200 installs. Rank climbs to #3 in Productivity.
- Week 12: Stable at ~400 organic installs/day. Feature has ended but ranking is self-sustaining.

**What made the mechanism fire.**
- Seeded enough initial installs to trigger ranking.
- Aggressive review collection maintained quality signal.
- Built on a new API (Agents) that platform-editors wanted to showcase.
- Gap-filled a real need (AI-generated rollup summaries) that Slack itself wouldn't prioritize.

**What could have broken it.**
- If reviews had averaged 3.9 instead of 4.8, the ranking loop would have stalled.
- If the onboarding took 4 minutes instead of 60 seconds, the editorial team would have passed.
- If he'd built for a mature API instead of Agents, the editorial angle would have been much weaker.
- If his email list had been 180 people instead of 1,800, the initial install seed would have been too small to trigger the loop.

contrast.explanation: Google's ranking mechanism and platform ranking loops are structurally cousins — both are algorithmic distribution engines a third party can ride. Three differences: (1) Goal alignment. Google ranks for answer quality; platform stores rank partly for answer quality and partly for platform-strategic reasons (showcasing new APIs, promoting high-margin app types). (2) Input signals. Google uses content quality, backlinks, user behavior. Platforms use installs, ratings, recency, and editorial discretion. (3) Feature-loop structure. SEO is continuous and algorithmic; platforms have discrete editorial features that act as step-function events on top of the algorithmic baseline. A useful heuristic: SEO rewards long-form, high-quality, backlink-rich content over months; platforms reward polished products, showcased platform capabilities, and initial install surges over weeks. Both compound, but the platform loop has a larger human-curation layer.

flashcards.front: Describe the platform ranking loop in six steps.
flashcards.back: (1) Initial installs from outside sources. (2) Installs + ratings + recency feed the ranking algorithm. (3) You move up in rankings. (4) More browsing users see you. (5) Organic installs increase. (6) Higher installs feed the algorithm further. Loop reinforces until a ceiling hits.

flashcards.front: Why are editorial features described as 'step-function events' rather than gradual lifts?
flashcards.back: A single feature produces a 10–100× install spike in a short window, and that spike often pushes the app into a higher ranking tier that persists after the feature ends — so the benefit is both the immediate spike and the sustained ranking change, not just the ad-equivalent value of the feature itself.

flashcards.front: Name the four reasons the 'build-early' strategy is structurally advantaged on new platforms.
flashcards.back: (1) Thinner competition — ranking loop fires with modest installs. (2) Free platform marketing — platforms promote themselves at launch and mention early apps. (3) Editorial hunger — editors need apps to feature and have a small candidate pool. (4) Broader API access — early-platform APIs are less restricted than mature ones.

flashcards.front: Gap-filling as a platform mechanism: you build the feature the platform's users need that the platform itself hasn't built (YouTube on ______, Bit.ly on ______, Imgur on ______).
flashcards.back: MySpace ; Twitter ; Reddit — in each case, the user-inserted content became a recruiting surface back to the gap-filling product.

flashcards.front: Name four conditions that cause the ranking-loop + features + gap-filling machine to break.
flashcards.back: (1) Platform saturation — too many apps, initial-install threshold becomes prohibitive. (2) Platform rule changes — APIs, categories, or policies shift and break the loop. (3) Your quality drops — ratings fall, loop reverses. (4) Gap gets filled natively — platform ships the feature you were providing.

scenarios.setup: A founder launched a Chrome extension six months ago. They've had 11,000 installs, a 4.2-star average rating, and rank #23 in their category for relevant search terms. They want to break into the top 10.
scenarios.question: Walk through what mechanisms they could pull on, ranked by leverage.
scenarios.modelAnswer: Rank by leverage: (1) Ratings quality. 4.2 is okay but not top-10 grade — most top-10 extensions rate 4.6+. Fix this first: instrument in-product prompts for rating (after a successful usage event, not upon install), reply to every negative review, and use the feedback to ship 2–3 small quality improvements. Raising the average to 4.6 alone can produce a ranking jump. (2) Install velocity. The ranking algorithm weights recent installs more than lifetime installs. Pushing a burst of 2,000+ new installs in a two-week window (via email list, PR push, or a paid creative test) will often move the needle more than a slow drip of 2,000 over six months. (3) Featured slot. Write a pitch to the Chrome Web Store editorial team showcasing what's unique or new about the extension. Editorial features deliver step-function ranking lifts and are free if you earn them. (4) Keyword tuning. Audit the listing title, short description, and detailed description. Make sure the primary target keyword appears in the title. Minor, but often a one-hour fix that moves ranking a few positions. (5) Gap-filling extension. If the extension solves a visible need on a major platform (e.g., a Twitter / X feature, a YouTube feature), create content on that platform showing the extension in action — each piece becomes a referring source. Rough ordering: quality fixes first (one month), install burst second (one month), editorial pitch third (ongoing), keyword tuning fourth (one afternoon). Expect 8–16 weeks to see the full effect of these together.
rubric: Identifies ratings as the highest-leverage lever given the current 4.2
Addresses install velocity (recency matters)
Recommends editorial feature pitch
Includes keyword/listing optimization
Suggests platform-native content as distribution
Provides realistic timelines

scenarios.setup: A founder is deciding between (a) spending 8 engineering weeks polishing their existing iOS app, or (b) spending 8 engineering weeks building for a newly-launched AR/VR headset platform with unknown success prospects.
scenarios.question: How should they think about the choice using the build-early mechanism?
scenarios.modelAnswer: This is exactly the build-early question Pachikov framed. Structure the choice on expected value, not certainty. (1) Probability the new platform succeeds. Most new platforms fail. Call it 1 in 5 (20%). (2) Upside if it succeeds. Being a day-one app on a successful platform can yield 10–100× the users of late entry. On a mature platform (iOS 2024), polishing an app produces incremental, not exponential, growth. Call day-one-on-winner = 50× expected users over 2 years; polishing = 1.5× expected users over the same period. (3) EV comparison. Polish = 1.0 × 1.5 = 1.5 user-units. New-platform bet = 0.2 × 50 + 0.8 × 0 = 10 user-units. (4) Other factors. Build-early on a flop is not zero value — the team learns a new stack, the engineering work has salvage in some cases. But it is mostly wasted. Nuance: the build-early bet only pays off if you can identify the 'reasonable guesses' about which platforms have a shot (Pachikov's language). The Apple Vision Pro, for instance, has better odds than most new platforms because of Apple's distribution and install base; a brand-new VR startup's platform has much worse odds. (5) Risk management. Don't bet the whole company on the new platform. If the 8 weeks would stop all other work, the downside case (flop-platform) hurts more than the EV math suggests. If it's 8 weeks of a single engineer while the main business continues, the bet is much more attractive. Recommendation: if the platform has even modest credibility and the 8 weeks don't stop core business, take the build-early bet. If the platform is unproven and the 8 weeks block other work, polish instead.
rubric: Frames as EV comparison, not certainty
Estimates realistic success probability for new platforms
Captures the order-of-magnitude upside asymmetry
Notes salvage value of flop-platform work
Distinguishes 'reasonable' from 'unreasonable' platform guesses
Accounts for opportunity cost on core business

pretest.prompt: Existing platforms has produced Instagram, Zynga, Evernote, YouTube, PayPal, and Airbnb — and has also been a dead end for thousands of apps that shipped to the App Store and disappeared. Before reading: what startup-shape and product-shape conditions predict which side of that distribution you'll land on?
pretest.modelAnswer: Three fit conditions. (1) Product-platform form factor. Your product must have a shape that naturally fits *inside* a platform — a mobile app, a browser extension, a marketplace listing, a bot, a widget, an integration. Products that don't have a platform-native form (e.g., a heavy B2B analytics dashboard) can't use the channel as a primary path. (2) Overlap between platform users and your ICP. The platform's user base must include a meaningful density of your target users. Shipping an enterprise DevOps tool to Craigslist is absurd; shipping it to GitHub Marketplace is a fit. (3) Competitive density and platform maturity. On saturated platforms, the ranking loop requires huge initial investment to trigger; on emerging or mid-maturity platforms, modest effort can achieve outsized ranking. Best fit: early-to-mid maturity platforms where your ICP is well-represented and your product form factor maps cleanly to the platform surface. Worst fit: mature platforms with thousands of competitors, where your product needs heavy customization to fit, or where your ICP isn't really there.

lesson.markdown:
## Three preconditions

For existing platforms to fit as a channel:

1. **Your product has a platform-native form factor.** It can live inside a platform surface — app, extension, bot, widget, listing, integration — without distorting the product.
2. **The platform's user base overlaps meaningfully with your ICP.** Shipping to a platform your customers don't use wastes effort regardless of platform quality.
3. **Platform maturity is low enough that the ranking loop is triggerable.** On emerging and mid-maturity platforms, modest initial installs can trigger the reinforcing loop. On saturated platforms, triggering requires multi-six-figure investment or a genuinely exceptional product.

## Where existing platforms fit strongly

- **Consumer mobile apps with a viral loop or social hook.** iOS App Store and Google Play. Games especially, but also photo/video apps, communication apps, and utilities. Zynga, Instagram, Snapchat, Clash of Clans — all rode app stores during a mid-maturity window.
- **Productivity and utility browser extensions.** Chrome Web Store, Firefox Add-ons. Smaller total volume than app stores but much thinner competition per install. Grammarly's early growth rode browser extensions heavily.
- **SaaS apps for popular host products.** Shopify apps, Salesforce AppExchange apps, Slack apps, Zoom apps, Atlassian apps. Highly targeted — users who browse the Shopify App Store own Shopify stores by definition. These marketplaces have lower absolute volume but the per-install conversion to paying customer is often 2–10× higher than paid channels.
- **Social/marketplace-routed consumer products.** Airbnb on Craigslist, PayPal on eBay, early Tumblr-embedded content sites. Work when the platform has a large audience with a specific unmet need and you can build an integration that routes users back to you.
- **Products designed for a new platform before it saturates.** The Evernote / Instagram / Trainyard pattern. Requires willingness to bet on platforms that might flop.

## Where existing platforms fit weakly

- **Enterprise B2B with multi-stakeholder buying cycles.** The platforms model rewards self-serve discovery and install; enterprise buyers don't discover tools through app stores. Exceptions: highly targeted marketplaces (Salesforce AppExchange) where the platform user *is* the buyer.
- **Products without a platform-native form factor.** A 12-screen B2B analytics dashboard is not going to fit in a Shopify app or a Chrome extension. Forcing it distorts the product and produces a worse experience on the platform than off.
- **Mature platform niches with saturated competition.** Launching a note-taking app on iOS in 2025 is much harder than in 2009. The ranking loop requires millions of installs to trigger in a category with thousands of competitors.
- **Products whose ICP doesn't use the platform.** Oil-and-gas procurement software does not belong in the Mac App Store.
- **Categories platforms actively restrict.** Some App Store policies constrain categories like crypto, adult content, certain health claims, parental controls. Check policy before committing.

## Platform-maturity spectrum and what fits at each stage

- **Brand-new (weeks to months old).** Thin users; rich editorial attention; broad APIs. Fit: bold experimental apps that showcase the platform's capabilities, even if the market isn't proven. High risk / high reward.
- **Emerging (1–3 years old).** Growing user base; still-accessible editorial; competition forming but not saturated. Fit: most strong consumer and SaaS apps. This is the sweet spot for the build-early strategy.
- **Mid-maturity (3–7 years old).** Established user base, well-known ranking mechanics, moderate competition. Fit: apps with a clear quality or positioning edge over existing competitors; specialized niche plays.
- **Saturated (7+ years old).** Huge competition, entrenched leaders, restricted APIs. Fit: only apps with exceptional quality, a very specific niche position, or a novel platform-feature hook (new API, new device integration).

## The cheapest diagnostic

Before committing engineering resources to a platforms play, run this three-question test:

1. **Form factor.** Can you describe your product as a platform-native artifact in one sentence? ('A Chrome extension that converts prices on any shopping site.') If you can't, the form factor isn't there.
2. **ICP overlap.** Can you name at least 3 concrete persona-journeys where the platform is where your target user would encounter your product organically? If you can only imagine ads driving them there, ICP overlap is weak.
3. **Maturity check.** Look up the current top 10 in your category on the target platform. How old are they? How polished are they? What would it take to crack top 20? If the top 10 are 5+ year-old incumbent products with 100k+ reviews, you're probably in a saturated niche and need exceptional differentiation.

If all three pass, existing platforms is likely the right Bullseye cycle. If any fail decisively, pick a different channel.

workedExample.setup:
Three founders evaluate fit:
- Ryo runs a $6/mo iOS mindfulness app, 8k users, 5-month average lifetime.
- Mei runs a $199/mo Shopify app for inventory forecasting. 40 customers, 19-month lifetime.
- Gus runs a $1,200/mo B2B compliance SaaS sold via 3-month enterprise sales cycles.
workedExample.walkthrough:
**Ryo (iOS mindfulness):** Weak-to-borderline fit despite apparent obviousness. Form factor (1) passes — it's an iOS app. ICP overlap (2) passes — mindfulness users are mobile-native. Maturity (3) fails hard — the mindfulness category on iOS is saturated: Calm, Headspace, and 50+ competitors with 100k+ reviews each. Triggering the ranking loop would require hundreds of thousands of installs — prohibitively expensive for an $6/mo app with $30 LTV. Recommendation: treat the App Store as a storefront, not a discovery channel. His real channel is probably social creative (TikTok / Instagram reels) — Ch 10. Revisit the platforms channel if a new iOS capability (say, a new meditation-surface API or Apple Vision Pro release) creates a temporary competitive opening.

**Mei (Shopify inventory app):** Strong fit. Form factor (1) passes — it's a Shopify app with a native-fit. ICP overlap (2) passes perfectly — every Shopify App Store browser is literally a Shopify store owner, and a meaningful subset has inventory-forecasting pain. Maturity (3) passes well — the Shopify inventory-forecasting category has 15–25 apps, not 500, and the top-ranked ones are 4.3–4.6 stars. Diagnostic: Mei can plausibly crack top 10 within 3 months with 300–500 installs and 50+ 5-star reviews. Her current 40 customers can jumpstart the review process. Recommendation: make Shopify App Store her next Bullseye cycle. Specific plan: convert 30 of her 40 existing customers into reviewers in week 1; use her email list to drive another 200 trial installs in weeks 2–3; pitch Shopify editorial for 'New & Notable' via their partner manager.

**Gus (enterprise compliance SaaS):** Weak fit. Form factor (1) fails — his product is a multi-stakeholder compliance dashboard with a 3-month sales cycle, SOC 2 negotiations, and on-premises deployment options. It doesn't fit inside an app store. ICP overlap (2) also fails — his buyers (Chief Compliance Officers at mid-market financial firms) do not browse app stores for tools. Maturity (3) is moot given (1) and (2). Recommendation: existing platforms is not his channel. His fit is sales (Ch 18) and BD (Ch 17), with content marketing (Ch 13) as a supporting long-tail play. Don't even investigate AppExchange unless a specific host-platform buyer pattern emerges.

**Key takeaway.** The platforms channel's fit is highly dependent on the triple of form factor, ICP overlap, and maturity. Mei passes all three decisively and should prioritize; Ryo's form factor and ICP look right but maturity rules him out; Gus fails on the first two. Absolute company size doesn't predict fit — a 40-customer SaaS (Mei) can have better platform fit than an 8,000-user consumer app (Ryo).

contrast.explanation: The mechanism concept explains the gears — how ranking loops, editorial features, gap-filling, and build-early combine to turn initial installs into compounding user acquisition. This fit concept operationalizes that theory into three empirical preconditions (form factor, ICP overlap, maturity) plus a 30-minute diagnostic any founder can run without writing code. The mechanism answers 'why could this work?'; fit answers 'will it work for this specific startup on this specific platform right now?' A startup can understand the mechanism perfectly and still misfit on all three preconditions; another startup can have great fit but fail because they don't understand how to trigger the mechanism.

You also need a platform-native form factor (a Facebook app, page, or integration your product can naturally fit) and acceptable maturity (Facebook platform apps as a channel is substantially deprecated vs. 2010; the ranking/feature dynamics that powered Zynga largely no longer exist). ICP on platform ≠ platform is the right channel.

scenarios.setup: A founder runs a $29/mo design tool for indie developers. LTV $580. They're evaluating whether to prioritize GitHub Marketplace, Chrome Web Store, or the Mac App Store as their next Bullseye cycle.
scenarios.question: Walk through their fit analysis across the three options.
scenarios.modelAnswer: Run the three-question diagnostic on each. **GitHub Marketplace.** Form factor (1) — a GitHub Action or App, probably yes, if the product can integrate with a repo workflow. ICP overlap (2) — strong; indie developers live on GitHub. Maturity (3) — moderate; GitHub Marketplace has ~1,500 apps total but less than 50 in design/UI categories. Verdict: strong fit, moderate effort to ship a well-scoped Action/App. **Chrome Web Store.** Form factor (1) — plausible if the tool has a browser-based workflow (e.g., a design-review helper that activates on Figma pages). ICP overlap (2) — moderate; many indie devs use Chrome but few design-tool installs convert to $29/mo purchases — Chrome users are price-sensitive. Maturity (3) — design extensions are moderately competitive (50+ decent competitors). Verdict: moderate fit; conversion concerns. **Mac App Store.** Form factor (1) — yes if it's a native Mac app. ICP overlap (2) — moderate; most indie devs are Mac users but don't browse the Mac App Store for dev tools. Maturity (3) — dev tools on Mac App Store are niche and relatively sparse but discovery is weak — most Mac-based dev tools market through Twitter, Hacker News, newsletters rather than the store. Verdict: weak fit; the channel is structurally underpowered for this ICP. Recommendation: GitHub Marketplace first. It has the strongest combination of ICP overlap and maturity, and offers a clear path to the ranking loop with 500–1,000 installs + good reviews. Second priority: Chrome Web Store as a lightweight secondary presence. Deprioritize: Mac App Store (right users but wrong discovery behavior).
rubric: Runs all three-question diagnostic on each option
Correctly identifies GitHub as the strongest fit
Notes Chrome Web Store conversion concerns
Identifies Mac App Store's weak discovery for developer tools
Prioritizes specifically rather than 'try all three'
Uses the form-factor/overlap/maturity framework throughout

scenarios.setup: A founder insists that the iOS App Store is their main channel because 'that's where mobile apps live.' Their category (habit tracking) has ~800 competing apps, many with 50k+ reviews. They've spent 6 months trying to climb rankings; current rank is #180 and not moving.
scenarios.question: Diagnose the misfit and recommend a pivot.
scenarios.modelAnswer: Primary diagnosis: saturated-platform misfit. The habit-tracking category on iOS is deeply saturated; ranking at #180 after six months suggests the ranking loop isn't triggering because initial install requirements are prohibitive relative to the founder's resources. The form factor (1) and ICP overlap (2) are fine — this is purely a maturity (3) failure. Sub-diagnoses: (a) The App Store has become a storefront rather than a discovery engine for this category. (b) Continuing to invest in App Store ranking will likely deliver marginal returns relative to other channels. (c) The category's winners (Streaks, Habitica, Productive) have >5 years of compounding advantage. Recommend pivot in three moves. First, reframe the App Store from channel to storefront — keep the app present and ASO-optimized but stop treating rank-climbing as a growth strategy. Second, test channels designed for saturated-category consumer apps: social creative (Ch 10 — TikTok/Reels habit-tracking demos), content marketing (Ch 13 — a habit-science newsletter or blog), viral loops inside the product (Ch 6 — friend-accountability mechanic that creates referrals). Third, consider a platforms play on a less saturated adjacent surface — e.g., an Apple Watch complication or a specific new-API feature that earns editorial attention even if the main App Store rank doesn't move. The worst thing to do is double down on iOS ranking for another six months; sunk cost suggests pivoting now.
rubric: Diagnoses saturation (maturity failure) specifically
Distinguishes storefront from channel
Recommends stopping rank-climbing investment
Prescribes 2–3 alternative channels suited to consumer saturated categories
Suggests platforms as adjacent-surface play (Watch, new APIs)
Names sunk-cost risk in continuing the current strategy

scenarios.setup: A founder is 3 weeks from launching a sophisticated financial-planning tool for small business owners. They're trying to decide between three existing-platforms bets: (a) launch as a Shopify app (tie it to ecommerce cash-flow planning), (b) launch as a QuickBooks App (sync with QBO), (c) launch as a general web app with a Chrome extension for scraping bank dashboards. Limited engineering bandwidth; can only pick one.
scenarios.question: Walk through the fit and tradeoff analysis.
scenarios.modelAnswer: Run diagnostic on each and compare strategic fit. **Shopify app.** Form factor (1) plausible if framed as ecommerce-cashflow-planning; slightly distorts the product which isn't ecommerce-specific. ICP overlap (2) moderate — Shopify merchants are a subset of small-biz owners but large subset. Maturity (3) strong — Shopify financial-planning apps are a mid-sized category (~60 apps). Tradeoff: ties product identity narrowly to ecommerce. **QuickBooks App.** Form factor (1) strong — QBO is where SMB financial data lives; a planning layer on top is natural. ICP overlap (2) very strong — QuickBooks online has 4–5M users who are almost perfectly the founder's ICP. Maturity (3) moderate — Intuit Marketplace has ~800 apps, ~30 in planning; competition real but not saturated. **Chrome extension.** Form factor (1) weak — financial planning isn't naturally a browser-extension use case; scraping bank dashboards is fragile and likely violates bank TOS. ICP overlap (2) weak — Chrome Web Store users are mostly consumers, not SMB finance owners. Maturity (3) moot given (1) and (2). Verdict: QuickBooks App is the clear winner. Best ICP overlap, strong form factor, acceptable maturity. The Shopify bet is second-best but artificially narrows the product. Chrome extension is a bad fit regardless of maturity. Additional consideration: QuickBooks App gives access to real financial data via Intuit's API, which likely makes the product dramatically better — so the channel choice is also a product choice. Recommendation: QuickBooks App, ship in 3 weeks, invest heavily in the 'featured app' pitch to Intuit, and plan to add Shopify App as a second marketplace only after QuickBooks has validated fit.

concepts.title: Core Tactics — Rankings, Gap-Filling, and Featured Slots

pretest.prompt: Once you've decided to pursue existing platforms as a channel, what specific plays actually move the needle? Before reading: what are the core tactics a founder should invest in, and how should they sequence them?
pretest.modelAnswer: Five core plays, roughly in order of sequencing: (1) App Store Optimization — the listing itself (title, keywords, screenshots, description, ratings flow) is the foundational asset. (2) Seed installs + ratings burst — using your email list, existing users, or a modest paid campaign to trigger the ranking loop. (3) Gap-filling integration design — build a feature whose insertion into the platform is itself a distribution surface (YouTube embeds on MySpace, Bit.ly links on Twitter, 'Post to Craigslist' at Airbnb). (4) Editorial feature pitches — identify the platform's editorial team, pitch polished one-pagers with Looms, align with current editorial themes. (5) Multi-app strategy — for companies with category fit, shipping multiple apps that each rank independently (Evernote Peek, Evernote Food) multiplies platform surface area. Sequencing: optimize the listing before seeding installs; seed installs before pitching editorial; pitch editorial before multi-app. Doing them out of order usually wastes effort.

lesson.markdown:
## Tactic 1 — App Store Optimization (ASO)

ASO is the minimum-viable use of the platforms channel. The listing itself is an asset that earns impressions every time a user browses the platform's discovery surfaces. Core levers:

- **Title.** Include your primary target keyword. The platform's ranking algorithm weights the title heavily. 'Focus Notes — Student Study Timer' will outrank 'Focus Notes' for the keyword 'study timer.'
- **Subtitle / short description.** Second-most-weighted field. Secondary keywords and a compelling one-line value proposition.
- **Screenshots.** Single largest lever on conversion rate (browse → install). The first screenshot should communicate the core value in under 2 seconds. A/B test if the platform supports it.
- **Description.** Less important for ranking than title/subtitle, more important for conversion. Write it for humans, not keyword-stuffing algorithms.
- **Ratings and reviews.** Ratings drive both ranking and conversion. Prompt for reviews at moments of positive experience (post-successful-action), not on launch.
- **Icon.** Small but compounding. Professional, platform-idiomatic iconography raises browse-to-click by 10–30%.

ASO alone won't drive rankings from nothing, but bad ASO caps how high good rankings can go.

## Tactic 2 — The initial install + ratings burst

The ranking loop requires initial momentum. Sources of that momentum:

- **Your email list.** The cheapest and most reliable. Send a launch-day email explicitly asking for installs AND ratings. A 5% install rate on a 2,000-person list produces 100 installs and 30–50 ratings — often enough to start moving.
- **Existing users of your main product.** Cross-promote a new app or extension to your current user base.
- **PR push.** One or two well-placed posts (Ch 7) can drive 500–5,000 installs.
- **Targeting blogs.** Placed content on relevant niche sites (Ch 16) routes highly qualified traffic.
- **Modest paid campaign.** $2,000–$10,000 on platform-native ads or broad social can jumpstart installs if other channels are weak.

The goal is not the installs themselves; it's the ranking shift that the installs produce. Once you rank top 20 in a category or keyword, organic discovery takes over.

## Tactic 3 — Gap-filling integrations

The highest-leverage platform plays are gap-filling integrations: features you build that users insert into the platform's own content, such that every piece of content becomes a distribution surface for you.

Examples:

- **YouTube's embed link on MySpace videos.** Every embedded video on a MySpace profile was a clickable recruitment link back to YouTube.
- **Bit.ly's shortened URLs on Twitter.** Every shared link was a Bit.ly-branded URL that educated users on the tool.
- **Airbnb's 'Post to Craigslist' feature.** Every Airbnb listing cross-posted to Craigslist included a backlink and brand touch.
- **PayPal's eBay auction inserts.** PayPal was inserted into eBay auctions as the payment method, acquiring users through sellers.
- **Canva's 'Made with Canva' footer.** Every free-tier export included a subtle attribution that routed viewers to Canva.

The design question: what's the smallest, most user-useful artifact your product can insert into the platform's native content flow? If the answer is 'nothing' — you may not have a gap-filling angle, and you're limited to rankings and features.

## Tactic 4 — Editorial feature pitches

Editorial features are step-function events worth pursuing explicitly. The best-practice play:

1. **Identify the editorial team.** App Stores, browser extension stores, Shopify, Slack, etc. all have partner / editorial contacts. LinkedIn and conference panels are common ways to find them.
2. **Time pitches to platform themes.** Editors plan around seasonal themes (back-to-school, productivity, holidays) and platform moments (new OS launch, new API release). Align your pitch with a current theme.
3. **Polish the listing first.** Editors won't feature sloppy listings. Fix ASO before pitching.
4. **Build for the feature, not just around it.** Showcase new platform capabilities — new APIs, new device features, new design paradigms. Editors prefer apps that make the platform look good.
5. **Ship a polished 2–3 minute Loom walkthrough.** Editors have limited time. A video showing quality in 90 seconds is worth a 10-page deck.
6. **Send a one-pager.** Product description, target user, 3–5 screenshots, key metrics (ratings, install velocity, MAU). Editors use this as internal-meeting material.

Most editorial pitches don't land. That's fine — a single feature can justify 20 failed pitches. Submit, follow up every 2–3 months, and treat the editorial relationship as an ongoing BD-style investment.

## Tactic 5 — Multi-app / multi-listing strategy

Evernote's 'multi-app strategy' is a force-multiplier for companies whose product spans multiple use cases: ship separate apps for each major use case so you rank in multiple categories. Evernote Food (food), Evernote Hello (contacts), Evernote Peek (study) — each earned its own rankings and editorial features, and each drove users back to the main Evernote ecosystem.

The trade-off: more apps = more engineering + design + support + reviews + quality maintenance. Only pursue if (a) your product genuinely spans use cases with different ICP segments, and (b) you have the engineering capacity to maintain quality across all of them. Shipping five mediocre apps rates worse than shipping one excellent one.

## Sequencing matters

Do these in order. ASO first (it's free and foundational). Then seed installs + ratings (trigger the loop). Then build gap-filling if it fits your product. Then pitch editorial (requires polished listing + decent metrics). Then consider multi-app (requires validated single-app channel fit). Skipping early tactics to jump to later ones is how founders spend engineering weeks on multi-app strategies while their core listing has 3-star ratings.

workedExample.setup: Sana is shipping a Chrome extension that auto-fills job applications from a resume. She has a 2,400-person email list of job-seekers from a free resume-review tool she ran last year. She has $4k budget for launch and one contractor designer.
workedExample.walkthrough:
**Week 1: ASO.** Sana writes a listing with title 'AutoApply — Job Application Autofill' (targets 'autofill' + 'job application' keywords). Short description: 'Autofill job applications on LinkedIn, Indeed, and 500+ career sites in 3 seconds.' She commissions 5 professionally-designed screenshots for $600 showing (a) the extension in action on a LinkedIn application, (b) Indeed, (c) a custom company site, (d) privacy / data-handling UI, (e) signup flow. She writes a 250-word description focused on the time-saved value prop. Icon is a clean, blue-and-white-themed pen-over-paper design.

**Week 2: Initial install + ratings burst.** She emails her 2,400-person list on launch day with subject 'I built something for us job-seekers — 3-second application autofill.' Opens: 48%. Installs: 280. She sets up in-extension prompts asking for a Chrome Web Store rating after the user has autofilled their 5th application. By end of week 2: 164 five-star ratings, 18 four-star, 4 three-star. Average: 4.8.

**Week 3: Keyword ranking.** She checks her Chrome Web Store rank for target keywords. 'Autofill job application' — ranked #6. 'Job application autofill' — ranked #3. 'Auto fill resume' — ranked #12. Initial loop is firing: organic installs from search now running 35/day.

**Week 4: Gap-filling design.** Sana considers: what artifact can AutoApply insert into LinkedIn/Indeed flows that would recruit new users? Two options: (a) an 'applied with AutoApply' invisible watermark (probably against LinkedIn TOS and ethically gray), (b) a referral-share flow that asks 'invite a friend who's also job hunting — get 1 month of premium features.' Option (b) is cleaner. Ships in week 4.

**Week 5: Editorial pitch.** Sana pitches the Chrome Web Store editorial team. Her one-pager: 'AutoApply — 4.8 stars, 2,400 installs in 30 days, solves a universal frustration (job application autofill), built on new Chrome extensions V3 manifest, privacy-first (all data local).' She ships a 2-minute Loom. First response: polite acknowledgment. She follows up in 8 weeks.

**Week 8: Ranking climb continues.** Organic installs now 55/day. Rank #3 in 'Autofill job application' is holding. Total installs: 3,600.

**Week 12: Editorial feature.** Chrome Web Store editors include AutoApply in a 'Back-to-Work Toolkit' themed collection for September. Installs spike to 320/day for 10 days, then settle at 95/day organic.

**Month 6: Multi-app consideration.** Sana considers shipping a second extension for 'AutoApply — Cover Letter Generator.' Her team is only her + a contractor. She decides against — maintaining one excellent extension is better than shipping two mediocre ones. Revisit if she hires a second engineer.

**What worked mechanically.** (1) ASO was done before install seeding, so the installs produced real ranking movement. (2) The ratings burst + sustained 4.8 average kept the loop reinforcing. (3) The editorial pitch had real metrics to reference (4.8 stars, healthy install velocity). (4) She resisted premature multi-app strategy. Sequencing was correct.

**What would have broken the plan.** If she'd skipped ASO, the 280 seed installs wouldn't have produced keyword rankings. If she'd built the cover-letter extension in month 2, quality would have suffered across both. If she'd pitched editorial in week 2 (before metrics existed), she'd have gotten ignored and wasted the contact.

contrast.explanation: SEO tactics (Ch 12) and platform tactics are cousins — both optimize for third-party algorithmic discovery. Key differences in execution: (1) ASO is much more template-driven than SEO. Platforms have rigid listing formats (title, subtitle, screenshots, description) while SEO content can take any form. (2) Ratings are central to platform rankings but don't exist in SEO. Platform tactics must explicitly design for ratings collection; SEO doesn't. (3) Editorial features are a platform-specific tool with no SEO equivalent — there's no human editor at Google who picks sites to feature. (4) Backlinks don't matter on platforms; SEO depends on them heavily. Rule of thumb: SEO tactics reward long-form content, backlink outreach, and topical authority; platform tactics reward polished listings, ratings velocity, and editorial relationships.

flashcards.front: Name the five core tactics for the existing-platforms channel in correct sequencing order.
flashcards.back: (1) ASO — listing optimization (title, subtitle, screenshots, description, icon, ratings flow). (2) Initial install + ratings burst to trigger the ranking loop. (3) Gap-filling integrations that insert your product into platform content. (4) Editorial feature pitches. (5) Multi-app strategy — only after single-app channel is validated.

flashcards.front: What are the five ASO levers that most affect ranking and conversion?
flashcards.back: (1) Title — primary keyword, weighted most heavily. (2) Subtitle / short description — secondary keywords and pitch. (3) Screenshots — biggest lever on conversion. (4) Description — for conversion, less so ranking. (5) Ratings and reviews — both ranking and conversion. Icon is smaller but compounding.

flashcards.front: Name four sources of initial installs to trigger the ranking loop.
flashcards.back: (1) Email list. (2) Existing users of your main product (cross-promo). (3) PR push (Ch 7) or targeting blogs (Ch 16). (4) Modest paid campaign ($2k–$10k). The goal isn't the installs themselves — it's the ranking shift they produce.

flashcards.front: Gap-filling integrations turn every piece of platform content into a distribution surface. Examples: YouTube's embed link on ______, Bit.ly's short URLs on ______, Airbnb's ______ ______ feature, PayPal's auction inserts on ______, Canva's 'Made with Canva' export footer.
flashcards.back: MySpace ; Twitter ; Post to Craigslist ; eBay — each insertion is a recruiting link back to the gap-filling product.

flashcards.front: Why should founders polish the listing BEFORE pitching editorial, not in parallel?
flashcards.back: Editors decide whether to feature based on what they see today. A sloppy listing gets ignored regardless of how good the pitch is. And editors rarely revisit a pitch they've already declined. Fix ASO first so the pitch is received favorably on first read; then pitch editorial from a strong position.

scenarios.setup: A founder's Shopify app has 4.3 stars, 180 installs after 4 months, and ranks #40 in its category. They ask: 'Should we spend $6k on paid social to drive more installs?'
scenarios.question: Walk through what they should invest in first, using the tactic sequencing framework.
scenarios.modelAnswer: Paid social is premature. Sequencing check: (1) ASO — is the listing optimized? 4.3 stars suggests ratings are soft; the listing itself should be audited first. A 4.3 rating caps ranking ceiling; most top-10 Shopify apps rate 4.7+. (2) Ratings burst — is the in-app ratings flow prompting at the right moments? 180 installs producing 4.3 stars is low review density; they need more reviews AND higher average. (3) Only then — initial install burst. The order-of-operations should be: first, spend $0 on ASO improvements (titles, screenshots, keywords). Second, spend $500–$1,000 on in-app ratings-collection improvements and direct outreach to existing users asking for reviews. Third, spend $2,000–$3,000 on a targeted install burst, ONLY after average rating is 4.6+ and ASO is tight. If they spend $6k on paid social now, the installs will produce mediocre rankings because the ratings are too low to reinforce the loop, and conversion rate on the listing will be weak because ASO is unoptimized. The $6k will be mostly wasted. Corrected plan: spend $0 this month on ASO + ratings work; $2k next month on install burst; hold remaining $4k for editorial pitch support and seasonal pushes.
rubric: Identifies paid social as premature given current ratings
Diagnoses 4.3 rating as a ceiling on ranking
Prescribes ASO and ratings work before install burst
Specifies order of operations with dollar amounts
Predicts wasted spend if they jump to paid social now
Reserves budget for editorial and seasonal pushes

scenarios.setup: A founder of a B2B SaaS with three planned apps — main app, mobile companion, AI assistant add-on — wants to ship all three simultaneously to 'maximize platform surface area.'
scenarios.question: What's wrong with this plan, and how should they sequence?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Shipping three apps simultaneously violates the sequencing principle of multi-app strategy: validate one first, then expand. Problems with simultaneous launch: (1) Engineering + design + support + review-response capacity spread thin → quality suffers across all three. (2) If ratings drop below 4.5 on any app, its ranking loop runs backward. (3) The founder won't learn which app fits best before doubling down. (4) Editorial teams are skeptical of founders who ship multiple apps simultaneously — it suggests lack of focus. Correct sequencing: (a) Ship the main app first. Polish ASO, drive initial installs, collect reviews, pitch editorial. (b) After 8–12 weeks of metrics, assess: is this single app achieving meaningful ranking? What's the conversion from install to paying customer? (c) Only if the main app is validated should the mobile companion or AI assistant ship, and each should ship as a separate focused launch. (d) Multi-app force-multiplier only activates when each app is genuinely excellent and has independent ranking. Evernote could ship Peek, Food, Hello because Evernote itself was stable and each companion app was genuinely polished. A startup shipping three half-finished apps simultaneously doesn't achieve Evernote's multi-app leverage — it achieves three mediocre listings that collectively drag down brand perception. Recommend: ship main app only. Revisit multi-app in 6 months.
rubric: Rejects simultaneous shipping
Identifies capacity-thinning as the core risk
Notes ratings cascade risk
Prescribes validate-one-first approach
References the Evernote precondition (validated main app)
Provides a specific revisit timeline

teachback.prompt: Explain the five core tactics for the existing-platforms channel with correct sequencing. Cover: (1) ASO and its key levers, (2) triggering the ranking loop via an initial install burst, (3) gap-filling integrations with examples, (4) editorial feature pitches and how to pitch well, (5) multi-app strategy and when it's premature, and (6) why sequencing matters.
rubric: All five tactics named with their purpose
ASO levers listed with specific fields
