pretest.prompt: Before you invest 6–12 weeks of engineering building for a new platform, how do you cheaply test whether that platform will be a productive channel for you? What are the minimum viable tests and the kill criteria?
pretest.modelAnswer: Three-tier cheap-testing approach. Tier 1 (1 day, $0): desktop research to confirm preconditions — is there a real category for your product on the platform? How many competing apps exist and what are their review counts? Are editorial features happening in your category? If basic preconditions fail, kill immediately. Tier 2 (1–2 weeks, $0–$500): minimum-viable listing or prototype — for mobile apps, a bare-bones MVP shipped to TestFlight / open beta; for extensions, a Chrome Web Store listing with a minimally-functional build; for marketplaces, a minimum-viable Shopify or Salesforce integration. Goal: measure whether submission + basic ASO produces any organic discovery at all. Kill criterion: <10 organic installs in 2 weeks after seeding 100 from email list. Tier 3 (4–8 weeks, $2k–$10k): full launch with seeded ratings burst and ASO optimization. Kill criteria: organic installs <50/week by week 6; average rating <4.3; no progress on keyword rankings for target terms. The overall principle: each tier gates the next. Don't skip Tier 1 to go straight to Tier 3.

lesson.markdown:
## Why cheap testing matters here specifically

Engineering effort to build for a new platform is expensive: typical browser extensions are 2–6 weeks, mobile apps 6–16 weeks, marketplace integrations 3–8 weeks. If the platform turns out to be a poor fit, that's 6–16 weeks of pure waste plus ongoing maintenance. Unlike paid ads (which you can kill in a day) or content marketing (which has salvage value as owned content), platforms-channel failures rarely have salvage. Cheap testing is about *de-risking the decision to build for the platform at all*.

## Tier 1 — Desktop research (1 day, $0)

Before writing any code, answer these with browser-only research:

- **Category existence.** Does a clear category for your product exist on the target platform? Check the platform's category taxonomy. If you can't find the right category, you'll struggle with discovery.
- **Competitive density.** How many competing apps rank in the top 20 of your category? Read the top 3's listings. What are their review counts and averages? Are they recent or 5+ years old?
- **Editorial activity.** Is the platform actively featuring apps in your category? Look at editorial collections, 'editor's choice,' 'new & notable' in the last 90 days. If your category gets zero editorial attention, that's a negative signal.
- **API and policy check.** Are there policy restrictions affecting your category? Does the platform's API support what you need to build? Does the platform take a cut that breaks your unit economics?
- **Ecosystem signals.** Do successful startups in your niche have meaningful platform presence? If every competitor ships to the platform and visibly invests there, it probably matters. If none ship there, that's a negative signal.

Kill criterion: if any of these fail decisively, stop here. Don't rationalize past a missing category or a platform policy that will force a product distortion.

## Tier 2 — Minimum viable submission (1–2 weeks, $0–$500)

For platforms where Tier 1 passes, ship the smallest possible product that can earn an organic install. Examples:

- **Chrome extension.** A 3–5 day build of the single most obvious feature; submit to Chrome Web Store with a decent listing; seed 50–100 installs from email list.
- **iOS app.** A TestFlight build that covers the single primary use case; TestFlight doesn't reach the public App Store but tests whether your design is shippable; paired with a 1-week App Store preview listing if you have budget for a real submission.
- **Shopify app.** A minimum-viable integration that handles the top 1–2 flows; submit to the Shopify App Store with a basic listing; seed from current email list.
- **Salesforce, Slack, or Atlassian marketplace.** Similar — minimum-viable integration, listing with ASO basics, seed from email list.

**What you're measuring.** (a) Does the platform's review process accept your product? (b) Once live, do you get *any* organic installs above what your seed should produce? (c) Are the early reviews positive enough to support a ranking loop? (d) Does keyword search work — do your target keywords actually surface your app in browsing?

Kill criterion for Tier 2: after 2 weeks with 100+ seeded installs and decent ASO, if organic installs from the platform's own discovery are <10, the platform's discovery isn't triggering for you. Either reconsider fit or invest heavily in Tier 3 to force a higher initial burst.

## Tier 3 — Full launch with optimization (4–8 weeks, $2k–$10k)

Tier 2 gate passed means organic discovery is producing at least a trickle — now you invest to turn the trickle into a flow.

- **Complete ASO.** Professional screenshots, optimized title/subtitle/description, icon refinement.
- **Large install burst.** 500–5,000 installs in a 2-week window via email, PR, paid, or a combination. Goal: hit a ranking threshold.
- **Ratings push.** In-app prompts at positive moments. Target 4.6+ average with 100+ reviews within launch month.
- **Editorial pitch.** Polished one-pager + 2-minute Loom. Send to editorial contacts.
- **Keyword tracking.** Monitor rankings for 5–10 target keywords weekly. Are they improving?

Kill criteria for Tier 3 (any one triggers reconsideration):

- Organic installs from platform discovery <50/week by week 6.
- Average rating <4.3.
- Keyword rankings haven't moved into top 30 for any target keyword by week 8.
- No editorial response after 2 follow-ups.
- Installs plateau and decline despite ongoing investment.

If all kill criteria avoided and at least one of them clearly passing (organic >100/week, rating >4.6, keyword rank in top 10), the channel is working and you can commit.

## Pre-commitment tests — list 10 platform users

A fast pre-Tier-1 sanity check: can you name 10 real people who would likely find your product through the platform's own discovery? If you're building a Shopify inventory app, can you name 10 Shopify merchants who browse the App Store monthly? If you can't, your ICP overlap assumption might be too abstract to be real.

## Anti-patterns to avoid

- **Building a full production app before any cheap testing.** Commit 3+ months of engineering without Tier 1 or Tier 2 signal.
- **Skipping ASO.** 'We'll optimize the listing later.' Later never happens, and Tier 2/3 metrics are distorted.
- **Treating a 'storefront deploy' as channel validation.** Getting accepted into the App Store is not the same as the platform being a productive channel.
- **Conflating seeded installs with organic.** The test is organic-discovery installs, not the ones you paid/emailed for.
- **Kill-criteria-blindness.** Defining kill criteria in advance is critical; founders who don't set them usually rationalize continuing investment past the point of diminishing returns.

workedExample.setup: Rafa is considering shipping his scheduling tool to the Zoom App Marketplace. Estimated engineering cost: 6 weeks of his + one engineer's time. Before committing, he wants to run a cheap test.
workedExample.walkthrough:
**Tier 1 (1 day).** Rafa opens the Zoom App Marketplace and audits:
- Category existence: Productivity / Scheduling. Yes, clear category exists.
- Competitive density: 12 apps in the scheduling subcategory. Top 3 have 800–2,400 reviews and 4.4–4.7 averages. 7–10 years old, with clear establishment. Category is moderately competitive, not saturated.
- Editorial activity: Zoom's editorial blog featured 2 scheduling apps in the last 90 days in 'recommended for remote teams' collections. Editorial attention exists.
- API and policy: Zoom's Marketplace API supports meeting creation, participant management, webhooks — sufficient for his scheduling use case. Zoom takes 20% cut of transactions through the marketplace; his pricing is outside that revenue model, so cut doesn't apply.
- Ecosystem signals: Two of his direct competitors have Zoom apps; one has 340 reviews averaging 4.5.

Tier 1 verdict: pass. Moving to Tier 2.

**Tier 2 (10 days, $0).** Rafa scopes a minimum-viable Zoom app: one-click 'schedule a Zoom meeting from my calendar tool,' and a basic Zoom-tab interface showing upcoming meetings. Skips advanced features (recurring meetings, team calendars, analytics). Uses his existing design system. 10 days of focused engineering produces a shippable v0.1.

He ships to the Zoom App Marketplace with a basic listing: title 'SchedSync — Scheduling for Zoom,' 3 screenshots he mocked up in Figma, a 200-word description, his icon. Submits for review. Zoom approval takes 6 days. Goes live.

He emails his 1,100-user existing SchedSync user base: 'I've built a Zoom integration — install it to sync meetings automatically.' 180 users install. He prompts for reviews; 41 five-star reviews come in over 5 days.

In the two weeks following launch:
- Seeded installs: 180.
- Organic installs (not from his email): 23.
- Ranking: moves from 'unranked' to #14 in scheduling subcategory by day 10.
- Keyword tracking: ranks #8 for 'calendar sync,' #12 for 'scheduling automation.'

Tier 2 verdict: organic discovery is triggering. 23 organic installs in 2 weeks is below the 'strong' threshold but above the 'dead' threshold. Kill criterion (>10 organic installs) passes. Moving to Tier 3.

**Tier 3 (8 weeks, $3k).** Rafa invests:
- Week 1: Professional screenshots ($800), rewrite title/subtitle/description, redesign icon ($400).
- Week 2: Install burst — Zoom-focused PR pitch to 3 remote-work newsletters ($0 outreach, $0 paid). Results: 2 newsletter placements, 400 new installs.
- Week 3: Second install burst via targeted LinkedIn content. 180 installs.
- Week 4: Ratings push. In-app prompts after first successful meeting. 165 new reviews, 4.7 average.
- Week 5: Editorial pitch. Loom + one-pager to Zoom's partnerships/editorial team. No response yet.
- Week 6 metrics check. Organic installs: 68/week. Average rating: 4.7 (230 reviews). Top keyword ranks: 'calendar sync' #4, 'scheduling automation' #3, 'meeting scheduler for Zoom' #2.
- Week 8: Editorial team responds, will consider for 'remote productivity' themed collection in 6 weeks.

Tier 3 verdict: all kill criteria avoided. Organic >50/week (68). Rating >4.3 (4.7). Keyword rank in top 10 (actually top 5 on multiple). Editorial response (positive). Channel committed.

**Total investment to validation:** ~7 weeks of engineering + $3k. **What the tiered testing prevented:** had he built a full-featured Zoom app (12 weeks of engineering) before any signal, and had Tier 1 failed (e.g., saturated category with no editorial activity), he'd have wasted 12 weeks. The tiered approach turned a 12-week, binary bet into a series of smaller bets with clear kill criteria.

contrast.explanation: Testing viral (Ch 6) and testing platforms are both channel-specific adaptations of shitty-click-through logic, but they differ in what you're measuring and what 'cheap' means. Viral tests measure k-factor — does a single user produce >1 additional user through invites? You can test this with a 100-user cohort over a week. Platform tests measure organic-discovery installs from the platform's own surfaces — you can't test this without actually being listed on the platform, which requires at least Tier 2 build + listing approval. Viral is testable with a fake door or a small cohort; platforms requires a minimum-viable shipped product. Because of that, platform testing takes longer (1–2 weeks minimum vs. 1 week for viral) and has a harder floor on cost ($500–$1,000 vs. potentially $0 for viral). The tiered-gate structure is the platforms-channel equivalent of the viral channel's k-factor measurement.

flashcards.front: Describe the three-tier cheap-testing approach for the platforms channel.
flashcards.back: Tier 1 (1 day, $0): desktop research on category, competition, editorial activity, API/policy, ecosystem. Tier 2 (1–2 weeks, $0–$500): minimum-viable submission; seed 100 installs; measure organic installs. Tier 3 (4–8 weeks, $2k–$10k): full launch with ASO, install burst, ratings push, editorial pitch. Each tier gates the next.

flashcards.front: Why does platforms testing have a harder floor on time and cost than other channels like paid ads or viral loops?
flashcards.back: You can't measure platform discovery without actually being listed. That requires a minimum-viable shipped product + listing approval (often 1–2 weeks), which caps how cheaply and quickly you can test. Failed platform tests also have little salvage value — unlike content or paid-ad creative, a failed app mostly stays failed.

flashcards.front: Name five Tier-3 kill criteria that should trigger reconsidering the channel.
flashcards.back: (1) Organic installs from platform discovery <50/week by week 6. (2) Average rating <4.3. (3) Keyword rankings haven't moved into top 30 for any target by week 8. (4) No editorial response after 2 follow-ups. (5) Installs plateau and decline despite ongoing investment.

flashcards.front: The Tier 2 kill criterion: after 2 weeks with 100+ seeded installs and decent ASO, if organic installs from the platform's own discovery are less than ______, the platform's discovery isn't triggering for you.
flashcards.back: 10 — below this threshold, the platform isn't delivering any native discovery and scaling investment won't change that unless something fundamental about the listing changes.

flashcards.front: A founder says 'we validated the Zoom App Marketplace as a channel because our app got accepted.' What's wrong with this?
flashcards.back: Acceptance is not validation. Getting accepted into the marketplace only proves the platform didn't reject your listing. Validation requires measuring organic installs from the platform's own discovery (Tier 2+) and, ideally, hitting positive kill-criteria in Tier 3 (organic >50/week, rating >4.6, keyword rankings in top 10). Conflating acceptance with channel-fit is a common anti-pattern.

scenarios.setup: A founder commits 14 weeks of engineering to build a flagship-quality iOS app for their B2B SaaS, skipping all cheap testing because 'we want to ship something polished.' After launch, organic installs are 3/week and ranking is stuck at #400 in their category.
scenarios.question: Diagnose what went wrong and what tiered testing would have caught earlier.
scenarios.modelAnswer: The founder violated the core principle of cheap testing: de-risk the decision before committing the investment. Specific failures tiered testing would have caught. (1) Tier 1 check (1 day) — category audit would have revealed the category is saturated with 5-year-old incumbents. Unranked apps need massive initial installs to crack top 50, and B2B SaaS on iOS rarely produces those installs organically. Early kill signal. (2) Tier 2 check (2 weeks) — a minimum-viable iOS build shipped to TestFlight + basic App Store listing would have produced metrics within 2 weeks: organic installs, keyword ranking, review quality. 3 organic installs/week at that scale suggests fundamental misfit. Could have killed at Tier 2 with ~$1k sunk, not 14 weeks. (3) ICP overlap — B2B SaaS buyers typically don't discover tools through the App Store. This is a classic platforms-channel form-factor mismatch. A fast Tier 1 would have raised this red flag immediately. Recovery options now: (a) Accept that the iOS app is not a channel — position it as a feature (mobile access for existing users) rather than an acquisition surface. Shift growth investment to sales, content, or a different platform like Salesforce AppExchange. (b) Pivot the iOS app's positioning to target consumer-adjacent use cases where App Store discovery works. (c) Retire the iOS app; absorb the cost as a learning. Don't double down on ranking-climbing — the category dynamics won't change. Cautionary framing: the 14 weeks is sunk. The decision now is not 'how to recover the sunk cost' but 'given where we are, what maximizes future growth?' Usually (a) or (c).
rubric: Identifies 'polish before test' as the core mistake
Walks through what Tier 1 and Tier 2 would have caught
Diagnoses B2B SaaS + iOS App Store as a fit mismatch
Offers 2–3 recovery options
Rejects double-down-on-ranking as a path forward
Applies sunk-cost framing to decision going forward

scenarios.setup: A founder is testing the Shopify App Store. At week 6 of Tier 3: organic installs 45/week, average rating 4.5, target keywords ranking #25–#40, editorial team non-responsive. The founder is wondering whether to kill, extend the test, or commit.
scenarios.question: What should they do, and why?
scenarios.modelAnswer: This sits in the gray zone — some kill criteria are close but none clearly fail. Analysis: organic installs (45/week) is just below the 50/week kill threshold — close but not clearly failing. Rating (4.5) is above the 4.3 kill threshold but below the 4.6 'strong' mark. Keywords (#25–#40) are above the top-30 threshold, barely, for some terms. Editorial non-response is a concern but not conclusive at 6 weeks. Three options: (a) Kill the test at 6 weeks. Aggressive, probably wrong — the metrics are borderline, and kill-criteria are designed to flag clear failures, not gray zones. (b) Commit now and scale investment. Premature — the metrics aren't clearly positive. (c) Extend the test 4 more weeks with targeted improvements. This is the best call. Specific targeted improvements: (1) Push average rating above 4.6 — audit recent negative reviews, identify 1–2 fixable product issues, ship improvements, and ask a second round of happy customers for reviews. (2) Narrower keyword focus — pick 2 winnable keywords (maybe #25 is within reach of top 10 with focused effort) and optimize listing copy for them. (3) Second editorial pitch with updated metrics. (4) Additional 200-install burst in one focused 1-week campaign. By week 10, the metrics should be clearly positive (>50 organic, >4.6, keywords in top 10) or clearly failing (similar or worse). At that point, commit-or-kill is straightforward. The principle: in gray zones, extend with targeted improvements, not with more of the same. If week 10 is still gray, accept the signal and pivot.
rubric: Recognizes this as a gray-zone case, not clear kill
Rejects both premature kill and premature commit
Prescribes targeted improvements over 'more of the same'
Names specific actions (ratings, keywords, editorial, install burst)
Sets a clear week-10 decision point
Articulates the gray-zone-extension principle

teachback.prompt: Explain how to test the existing-platforms channel cheaply before full commitment. Cover: (1) why cheap testing matters especially here (engineering cost, low salvage), (2) the three tiers with cost/time/goals for each, (3) kill criteria at each tier, (4) what you're actually measuring (organic-discovery installs, not seeded), and (5) common anti-patterns to avoid.
rubric: All three tiers with their time, cost, and goals

concepts.title: Pitfalls of the Existing Platforms Channel

pretest.prompt: Companies have built billion-dollar businesses on existing platforms (YouTube, Instagram, Zynga, Evernote) and have also been destroyed by them (the thousands of Facebook-platform apps wiped out in 2010–2012, the dozens of Twitter clients killed by API changes, the Zyngas whose post-Facebook independence never materialized). Before reading: what are the specific failure modes founders need to anticipate, and what are the preventive measures?
pretest.modelAnswer: Six major pitfalls. (1) Platform risk — the platform owner changes APIs, rankings, revenue splits, or policies and eliminates your channel or product overnight. Preventive: diversify channels early; don't let a single platform exceed ~60% of acquisition. (2) Platform lock-in that prevents direct customer relationships — users install your app but you never get their email. Preventive: collect emails early and funnel users to direct-owned channels. (3) Chasing vanity rankings — optimizing for #1-in-category status that doesn't translate to revenue or retention. Preventive: measure install-to-active and active-to-paid conversion, not ranking position. (4) Platform saturation blindness — betting on a platform that's already saturated. Preventive: run Tier 1 diagnostic first. (5) Multi-app premature scaling — shipping multiple apps before one is validated. Preventive: validate single-app fit first. (6) Underinvesting in quality — assuming initial installs will compensate for mediocre reviews. Preventive: treat ratings as the ranking ceiling, not a side metric.

lesson.markdown:
## Pitfall 1 — Platform risk

This is the single largest pitfall and the one founders most often underestimate. The platform owner can change your economics at will:

- **API changes.** Twitter restricted its API in 2012 and then again in 2023, killing or severely impairing dozens of third-party clients. Facebook's graph API shutdown in 2015 eliminated entire categories of Facebook-platform apps overnight.
- **Ranking algorithm changes.** App Store search algorithm updates regularly reshuffle category rankings. Chrome Web Store has repeatedly deprioritized certain extension categories (remote desktop, cookie managers) for policy reasons.
- **Revenue-split changes.** Apple's 30% cut became contentious with the Epic Games lawsuit; Apple has altered small-developer rates since. Shopify's Apps Store revenue terms have evolved.
- **Policy enforcement.** Apps can be removed from stores for policy violations, sometimes without clear notice or appeal.
- **Deplatforming.** In extreme cases, platforms remove apps entirely.

**Preventive measure:** diversify channels before the platform is your only channel. Once you have platform-driven scale, actively invest in channels you own (SEO, email, direct traffic, content) so no single platform decision can kill your business. A rule of thumb: if any single platform exceeds 60% of your acquisition, you're in dangerous territory — build the next channel now.

## Pitfall 2 — Lock-in that prevents direct relationships

Platforms often prevent direct customer relationships. Apple doesn't give you an email for iOS users. Chrome doesn't share user data beyond the install. Salesforce's AppExchange gatekeeps some customer contact.

**Consequence:** you may have hundreds of thousands of users with no way to contact them outside the platform. When the platform deprioritizes you, you have no recovery path.

**Preventive measure:** design the product to pull users into a direct relationship early. Require or encourage account creation with email. Incentivize newsletter signup. Offer premium features via a direct (non-platform) flow. The Evernote model: users install via the App Store but are then funneled into Evernote's own account system and communication channels. Strong platforms founders treat the platform as a top-of-funnel and immediately move users into their own stack.

## Pitfall 3 — Chasing vanity rankings

Ranking #1 in a category is satisfying but not inherently valuable. What matters: installs from that ranking, conversion from install to active user, conversion from active to paying customer.

Common anti-pattern: founders spend $30k on install campaigns to hit #1 in a category, briefly celebrate, then realize the campaign-driven installs are low-intent and churn immediately. Meanwhile their paid-ads CAC was already better than the effective CAC of the campaign.

**Preventive measure:** measure channel success by downstream metrics — install-to-trial, trial-to-paid, paid-retention — not by ranking position. If a platform is working, rankings will matter; if rankings aren't translating to paying customers, the platform isn't actually a fit and rank-chasing is wasted motion.

## Pitfall 4 — Saturation blindness

Founders often pick platforms based on absolute audience size (App Stores have billions of users!) without accounting for competitive density. A saturated category inside a huge platform is worse than an emerging category inside a smaller one — rankings depend on *relative* performance, not absolute.

**Preventive measure:** Tier 1 diagnostic always. Audit competitive density before committing. Note especially: if the top 10 in your category are 5+ years old with 100k+ reviews each, competing at rank is very expensive. Consider a narrower category or an adjacent platform where your app would be one of few rather than one of thousands.

## Pitfall 5 — Multi-app premature scaling

Evernote's multi-app strategy worked because Evernote itself was a proven, excellent product before Peek, Food, Hello shipped. Startups that jump to multi-app before validating a single app usually end up with 3–5 mediocre apps that drag down brand perception.

**Symptoms of premature multi-app:**
- Reviews on the main app average <4.5.
- Editorial team hasn't featured any app yet.
- Engineering team is already stretched maintaining the main app.
- Multi-app pitch is 'more surface area' rather than 'distinct use cases with distinct ICPs.'

**Preventive measure:** validate single-app first. Only expand when: (a) main app ratings are 4.6+, (b) main app has meaningful organic ranking, (c) the second app addresses a clearly distinct use case or ICP segment, (d) engineering capacity supports quality at scale.

## Pitfall 6 — Underinvesting in quality

Platform rankings compound quality. A 4.8-star app outranks a 4.3-star app with the same install count, because the algorithm weights ratings heavily. And editorial features go to high-quality apps almost exclusively.

**Anti-pattern:** founders treat reviews as a side metric and focus on installs. Then they wonder why the app plateaus at #80 despite heavy investment.

**Preventive measure:** treat ratings as the ranking ceiling. Invest in product quality, in-app ratings flows, and review-response as first-order activities. A 4.3-star average can never rank top 10 in a competitive category; a 4.7+-star average can even with modest install volume.

## Pitfall 7 — Product distortion for platform fit

Sometimes founders contort their product to fit a platform's surface — building a Chrome extension that doesn't really need to be an extension, or a Slack app that doesn't really leverage Slack. The platform presence delivers marginal installs while the contorted product confuses the core positioning.

**Preventive measure:** before building for a platform, articulate in one sentence why this product *belongs* on this platform specifically. If you can't, the platform fit isn't there and you should skip it.

## Pitfall 8 — Ignoring kill-criteria in ongoing investment

Initial channel-testing defines kill criteria, but founders sometimes forget to revisit them as investment scales. A channel that was working at $2k/mo may not be working at $20k/mo, but founders anchor on the early success and scale past the point of positive marginal return.

**Preventive measure:** quarterly re-assessment of the channel's economics at current scale. Are organic installs still growing? Is ranking still improving? Is CAC from platform-driven acquisition still favorable? If any answer is no and the trend is steady or worsening, pause scaling.

workedExample.setup: Noor runs a 3-year-old productivity app on iOS. 180k MAU, 65% of acquisition from App Store organic discovery, $950k ARR. Recently, Apple updated its search algorithm; her app dropped from #4 to #28 in its category. Organic installs dropped from 720/day to 180/day. She needs to diagnose and recover.
workedExample.walkthrough:
**Platform risk assessment.** Noor's situation is the canonical platform-risk pitfall: 65% of acquisition depends on a single platform whose algorithm just changed. The $950k ARR isn't under immediate threat (existing users keep paying) but new-user growth has been cut 75%.

**Immediate diagnostic.**
- Cause of ranking drop: Apple's algorithm appears to now weight recent install velocity more heavily. Her app's growth had plateaued, so recent velocity signals are weaker than competitors shipping new releases.
- Review health: stable 4.6 average, 34k total reviews. Not the cause.
- Keyword health: lost rank on primary keywords but retained rank on long-tail terms.

**Short-term response (next 30 days).**
1. Trigger a ratings campaign — in-app prompts for recent happy users, push to hit 4.7 average. Small but directionally correct.
2. Ship a visible update with new features — Apple's algorithm may reward recent-update signals.
3. Pitch Apple editorial team with the update for potential featured-slot inclusion.
4. Run a $15k paid install campaign to inject recent install velocity into the algorithm.

**Medium-term response (next 90 days).**
1. Recognize that 65% reliance on App Store is structurally dangerous regardless of recovery. Diversify channels now.
2. Launch investment in content marketing (Ch 13) — SEO-targeted productivity articles. This won't produce results for 6–12 months but starts the clock.
3. Implement email capture in the app — funnel users into Noor's direct email list so future platform disruption has less impact on LTV.
4. Consider launching on Android / other platforms. Having multiple platform surfaces reduces single-platform risk.

**Long-term structural change (next 12 months).**
1. Target: reduce App Store's share of acquisition from 65% to <40%.
2. Build out content, email, referral, and potentially paid-social channels to carry the other 60%.
3. Invest in direct-to-user relationships — web-based account management, email communication, community features.

**What Noor should have done earlier.** This situation is a preview of the pitfall-preventive measures she skipped: (a) she let a single platform exceed 60% of acquisition without backup channels; (b) she didn't invest in direct user relationships until forced; (c) she was measuring by ranking rather than by the breadth of her acquisition portfolio. The current crisis is her recovery opportunity — but the lesson is to never again let a single platform have this much power over the business.

**What's salvageable.** Because she has 180k MAU and strong ratings, she can likely recover ranking over 3–6 months with the short-term actions. But recovery isn't the real win; diversification is. If she uses this crisis to build a 3-channel acquisition stack, she'll be structurally healthier in 12 months than she was before the algorithm change — even if App Store returns to normal.

contrast.explanation: Core tactics explain the plays that build the channel up; pitfalls explain the failure modes that tear it down. Both operate within the same channel but on different phases. Tactics focus on ASO, install bursts, gap-filling, editorial, and multi-app — all about making the channel work. Pitfalls focus on platform risk, lock-in, vanity metrics, saturation, premature multi-app, quality under-investment, product distortion, and kill-criteria neglect — all about preventing the channel from destroying you. A useful framing: tactics are what you do; pitfalls are what you guard against while doing them. Mastery requires both — the best channel operators simultaneously execute the positive plays and maintain explicit awareness of what could kill the channel.

flashcards.front: Name the six major pitfalls of the existing-platforms channel.
flashcards.back: (1) Platform risk (API, ranking, revenue, policy changes). (2) Lock-in preventing direct customer relationships. (3) Chasing vanity rankings. (4) Saturation blindness. (5) Multi-app premature scaling. (6) Underinvesting in quality. Plus (7) product distortion for platform fit and (8) ignoring kill-criteria as investment scales.

flashcards.front: What's the 60% rule for platform risk, and what's the preventive action?
flashcards.back: If any single platform exceeds 60% of your acquisition, you're in dangerous territory. Preventive action: actively invest in channels you own (SEO, email, direct traffic, content) so no single platform decision can kill your business. Ideally drop single-platform share below 40–50% before scaling commitments.

flashcards.front: Why are ratings 'the ranking ceiling'?
flashcards.back: Platform ranking algorithms weight ratings heavily; a 4.3-star app cannot rank in the top 10 of a competitive category no matter how many installs it has, while a 4.7-star app can rank with modest install volume. If you underinvest in product quality and review-response, you cap how high rankings can ever go — and the ceiling is usually far below where editorial features happen.

flashcards.front: Lock-in pitfall preventive: design the product to pull users into a ______ ______ early. Require or encourage account creation with email. Offer premium features via a non-platform flow. The ______ model (App Store → their own account system) is the archetype.
flashcards.back: direct relationship ; Evernote — treat the platform as top-of-funnel, immediately move users into your own stack.

flashcards.front: A founder ranks #1 in their category on iOS but has low conversion from install to paying customer. What pitfall is this, and what should they measure instead?
flashcards.back: Vanity-ranking pitfall. Ranking is an input, not an outcome; installs from low-intent browsers don't convert to paying customers. Measure downstream metrics: install → trial, trial → paid, paid → retention. Ranking only matters to the extent it produces profitable paying customers — if it doesn't, rank-chasing is wasted motion regardless of how high the rank goes.

scenarios.setup: A 2-year-old consumer app has 1.2M installs, 420k MAU, $3.2M ARR, and 82% of acquisition from App Store organic discovery + occasional featured slots. The founder is proud of the 'capital-efficient growth.' An investor asks whether they have concerns about concentration risk.
scenarios.question: What's the structural critique, and what should the founder do?
scenarios.modelAnswer: The structural critique: 82% reliance on a single platform is a severe concentration risk that positive metrics mask. Every positive indicator (installs, ARR, editorial features) amplifies the downside if Apple changes anything about the algorithm, the revenue split, or the category's treatment. The investor is right to be concerned. Specific risks: (1) Algorithm changes — a 30% ranking drop (historically plausible) could cut organic installs by 60–80%, crushing new-customer growth for 6–12 months. (2) Apple policy — Apple could restrict the app's category (historical examples: parental controls, crypto, adult content). (3) Featured-slot dependence — editorial features are discretionary and can stop without warning. (4) Revenue split — Apple's 30% cut (reduced for small developers) is politically under pressure but not stable in either direction. What the founder should do: (a) Recognize that current growth is genuinely capital-efficient AND structurally dangerous; both are true. (b) Invest aggressively in channels-you-own: start a content-marketing engine (Ch 13), launch an email capture funnel that funnels app users into owned newsletters, build referral mechanics (Ch 6) that produce growth independent of App Store. (c) Set a 12-month goal to reduce App Store share of acquisition from 82% to <60%. (d) Diversify onto Android if not already — two-platform risk is lower than single-platform risk. (e) Build the financial cushion — a quarter of operating capital should be reserved for the downside scenario. This isn't a request to reduce App Store investment; it's a request to invest in other channels alongside it.
rubric: Validates the investor's concern
Names specific platform-change risks
Acknowledges capital-efficiency and risk are both true
Prescribes channel diversification with specific channels
Sets a measurable diversification target
Addresses financial cushion / downside preparation

scenarios.setup: A founder pivots their SaaS to include a Slack app because 'Slack is where our users are.' The Slack app is a lightweight wrapper of existing features — it adds no Slack-native value. Installs are slow. The team is considering doubling down with editorial pitches and install campaigns.
scenarios.question: Diagnose the underlying pitfall and recommend the correct path.
scenarios.modelAnswer: The underlying pitfall is product distortion for platform fit. The founder built a Slack app because 'users are on Slack,' not because the product genuinely belongs on Slack. A lightweight wrapper that adds no Slack-native value violates the precondition — if you can't articulate why this product belongs on this platform specifically, the platform fit isn't there. Why the current plan won't work: (1) Slack editorial teams feature apps that showcase platform capabilities (Canvas, Workflow Builder, Agents APIs, etc.). A wrapper showcases nothing; editorial will decline. (2) Install campaigns can produce installs but low conversion and low ratings, because the app doesn't do anything natively useful. Low ratings cap ranking; ranking caps organic. The strategy is structurally incapable of producing sustainable growth. Correct path options: (a) Kill the Slack app. Acknowledge sunk cost, redirect engineering to channels that actually fit. (b) Genuinely rebuild for Slack. Identify a specific Slack-native workflow — e.g., a channel-bot that posts contextual data in response to commands, an Agent that summarizes relevant messages. Make the Slack version fundamentally better for Slack-native use than the web app. This requires 6–12 weeks of real Slack-specific design, not wrapper work. (c) Reposition the Slack app as a feature, not a channel — keep it live for existing users who want Slack access, but don't invest in install growth. Best option is (c) in the short term + (b) in the medium term if Slack is genuinely strategic. Worst option is continued investment in the current wrapper. The founder should articulate in one sentence why their product belongs on Slack specifically. If they can, build for that sentence. If they can't, withdraw.
rubric: Identifies product-distortion-for-platform-fit as the pitfall
Explains why editorial + install campaigns won't work on a wrapper
Provides 3 distinct path options
Recommends withdrawing or rebuilding rather than doubling down
Names the 'one-sentence belonging' test
Treats sunk cost correctly (irrelevant to forward decision)

scenarios.setup: A Shopify app founder celebrated hitting #3 in their category last month by spending $22k on paid installs. This month, rank dropped back to #18 and their install economics are -$0.40 per install net of payback.
scenarios.question: What pitfall is this, and what should they do?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Classic vanity-ranking pitfall combined with ignoring-kill-criteria. The founder optimized for #3 ranking, which is an input, not an outcome. The downstream economics show the ranking wasn't translating to profitable paying customers (-$0.40/install net of payback means they're losing money on the installs that ranking produced). The #3 rank was a temporary achievement purchased at negative ROI. What they should do: (1) Stop the paid-install campaign immediately. It's losing money and not producing durable value. (2) Re-audit the channel on downstream metrics — install-to-paid conversion, paid-to-retention, LTV — and compare to other channels they could be investing in. (3) Accept that the 'rank #3 milestone' is not a real win. Rankings are valuable only to the extent they produce profitable customers. (4) Investigate WHY conversion from install to paid is weak: is the app underperforming on value? Is the marketplace audience wrong? Is the price wrong? Is the onboarding broken? Fix the underlying conversion problem, not the symptom (ranking). (5) Revisit kill-criteria with fresh eyes: if this channel can't achieve positive unit economics at rank #20 (typical organic level), it probably shouldn't be prioritized. Budget shifts to channels with better unit economics. (6) Treat the $22k as a lesson on how to measure channel success: downstream metrics, not rankings. Future test budgets should have explicit 'kill if net unit economics are negative at month 2' criteria, and the founder should hold themselves to them even when rankings are moving.
rubric: Identifies vanity ranking AND kill-criteria pitfalls
Recommends stopping the campaign immediately
Prescribes downstream-metric auditing
Investigates the underlying conversion weakness
Explicit kill-criteria for future investment
Frames the $22k as a lesson, not a loss to recover

teachback.prompt: Explain the major pitfalls of the existing-platforms channel. Cover: (1) platform risk and the 60% rule, (2) lock-in preventing direct relationships (Evernote model as antidote), (3) chasing vanity rankings vs. measuring downstream, (4) saturation blindness, (5) multi-app premature scaling, (6) quality underinvestment and ratings as the ranking ceiling, (7) product distortion for platform fit, and (8) ignoring kill-criteria as investment scales.
rubric: All six primary pitfalls named with mechanism
Preventive measures specified for each
Concrete historical examples (Twitter API, Facebook graph, etc.)
Rule-of-thumb thresholds given (60% rule, rating ceiling)
